title: Radioactive Materials Licensing and OSHA
narrationScript: Two regulators stand over every radiography job, and this lesson covers both. Industrial radiography is never done under a general license. Your company files NRC Form 313, or the Agreement State equivalent, and under 10 CFR 34.13 the application must lay out the entire safety program: radiographer training and certification verification, written operating and emergency procedures, job performance inspections at least every six months, the organizational structure, the RSO's qualifications including two thousand hours of hands-on experience, leak testing, instrument calibration, and every location of use and records. Those statements come back as binding license conditions — the application becomes the license. Amend before you change anything it describes, renew at least thirty days before expiration, and remember the license cannot be transferred. OSHA owns the rest of the site: falls, confined spaces, electrical work, chemicals. Its radiation rule, 29 CFR 1910.1096, still carries quarterly limits from another era — but comply with Part 20 under your license and you are deemed in compliance for licensed material. Machines remain OSHA's territory.

sections.heading: Two Rulebooks on Every Job
sections.body:
A radiography crew answers to two federal frameworks at once. The **radioactive materials license** governs everything radiological: which sources the company may possess, who may expose them, the procedures they follow, and the records that prove it. The **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** governs the workplace wrapped around that source — the scaffold, the excavation, the vessel you crawl into, the chemicals in the darkroom.

This lesson walks both halves. First, what it actually takes to obtain and keep a specific radioactive materials license for industrial radiography: the application package of **10 CFR 34.13**, the review standards of **10 CFR 30.33**, and the conditions that follow the license for its entire life. Second, where OSHA fits — a different agency, a different statute, and a boundary with the NRC that every radiographer should be able to draw from memory.

Hold one idea through the whole lesson: **regulators divide the hazard, not the job site.** The same shot on the same night can involve the materials license (the gamma source), the state machine registration (the X-ray crawler), and OSHA (the fall protection on the pipe rack) — and an inspector from any of them can show up.

sections.heading: Only a Specific License Will Do
sections.body:
The Atomic Energy Act framework recognizes two kinds of byproduct-material licenses. A **general license** is granted by the regulation itself — no application, no review — for low-hazard uses such as certain fixed gauging devices under 10 CFR Part 31; the user often acquires it simply by purchasing the device. A **specific license** exists only after a company files an application, the regulator reviews it, and a license document is issued to a named licensee. Industrial radiography sits far above the general-license line: **it may be conducted only under a specific license**, issued by the NRC under 10 CFR Part 34 or by an Agreement State under its compatible regulations. Even the reciprocity general license of 10 CFR 150.20 from the last lesson exists only because a specific license stands behind it.

The application goes in on **NRC Form 313** — Agreement States use equivalent forms — and the NRC's program-specific guidance for preparing it is **NUREG-1556, Volume 2**. Before granting the license, the regulator applies the general standards of **10 CFR 30.33**: the use is authorized by the Act, the applicant's **equipment and facilities are adequate** to protect health and minimize danger to life or property, the applicant is **qualified by training and experience** to use the material safely, and the special requirements of Part 34 are satisfied. Application and annual fees — 10 CFR Parts 170 and 171 for NRC licensees — keep the regulatory program funded by the industry it oversees.

sections.caption: The application package for a specific industrial radiography license — what 10 CFR 34.13 requires the applicant to submit.
rows: Training program for radiographers and radiographers' assistants
Classroom and hands-on training, examinations, and annual refresher training
How radiographer certification by a certifying entity (IRRSP-type) is verified, documented, and kept current
Step-by-step rules for normal operations and for source emergencies
Each radiographer and assistant observed at intervals not exceeding 6 months
The radiation-safety chain of command, with delegations of authority and responsibility in writing
Radiographer-level training and exams, 2,000 hours of hands-on experience as a qualified radiographer, and formal training in managing a radiation safety program
Procedures, instruments, and analyst qualifications; sealed sources are leak tested at intervals not exceeding 6 months
Methods and the calibrator's experience; instruments calibrated at intervals not exceeding 6 months and after servicing
Locations of use and of records
Every field station and permanent radiographic installation, and where required records will be maintained

sections.caption: From application to license: the 10 CFR 34.13 package goes in on NRC Form 313, is reviewed against 10 CFR 30.33, and comes back as binding conditions on the license — kept alive by amendment before change and by timely renewal.

sections.heading: Living Under the License
sections.body:
What comes back from the regulator is a short document — the NRC version is **Form 374, Materials License** — and every line of it is enforceable. A radiography license names the **licensee** and its addresses; the **radionuclides** authorized (typically Ir-192, Co-60, or Se-75, plus **depleted uranium** as device shielding); the **sealed-source and exposure-device models** the company may use, each an approved design listed in the **Sealed Source and Device Registry**; the **maximum activity** the licensee may possess at any one time; the **authorized places of use and storage**; the **Radiation Safety Officer by name**; an expiration date; and a numbered list of conditions. Live under it like this:

- **Amend first, change second.** A new RSO, a new device model, more activity, a new permanent installation or field station — each requires a license amendment, filed like the original application, **before** the change is made.
- **Renew on time.** NRC radiography licenses typically run **10 years**; Agreement State terms vary. File renewal **at least 30 days before expiration** and the existing license remains in effect under the timely-renewal rule until the regulator acts.
- **The license is not transferable.** A corporate sale or change of control requires the regulator's prior written consent.
- **Security rides along.** Typical radiography sources exceed the Category 2 threshold — for Ir-192, 0.8 TBq, about 22 curies — so **10 CFR Part 37** applies: trustworthiness-and-reliability screening for unescorted access, access controls, and source tracking.

sections.title: The application becomes the license
sections.body: A standard condition incorporates the **statements, representations, and procedures** of your application — and of every amendment and renewal — into the license itself. The operating procedure your RSO wrote and submitted is now enforceable law for your crews: an inspector who finds field practice drifting from the submitted procedure is documenting a **license violation**, even if no dose limit was ever threatened. Keep the binder and the behavior identical, and when a better way of working emerges, change the document by **amendment first** — never by quiet drift.

sections.heading: OSHA: The Other Inspector on Your Job
sections.body:
The **Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970** created OSHA inside the **U.S. Department of Labor**, with a mandate covering nearly every private-sector employer in the country — radiography companies included. Two mechanisms do the work. **Standards** — 29 CFR 1910 for general industry and 29 CFR 1926 for construction, and a radiography crew works under both depending on the site — set specific rules. Where no standard exists, the **General Duty Clause, Section 5(a)(1)**, still requires every employer to furnish employment free from recognized hazards that are causing or likely to cause death or serious physical harm.

Look at a night shot through OSHA's eyes and the radiation is almost the least of it: **fall protection** on the pipe rack, ladder and scaffold rules, **permit-required confined-space entry** (29 CFR 1910.146) before you crawl into the vessel, **electrical safety** around the generator, **hazard communication** (29 CFR 1910.1200) for darkroom chemistry, PPE, heat, and noise. Industry experience is blunt: conventional hazards injure far more radiographers than radiation does.

Administration matters too. Employers log recordable injuries, report a work-related **fatality within 8 hours** and any in-patient **hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye within 24 hours**, and post the OSHA notice of worker rights. Roughly half the states operate OSHA-approved **State Plans**, which must be at least as effective as federal OSHA. And **Section 11(c)** protects workers from retaliation for raising safety concerns — the OSH Act's parallel to the NRC's own employee-protection rules.

sections.heading: OSHA and Radiation: Old Numbers, Clear Boundary
sections.body:
OSHA has its own ionizing radiation standard for general industry — **29 CFR 1910.1096** — and reading it is a trip back in time. Adopted from pre-1971 Atomic Energy Commission rules and never modernized, it sets **quarterly** limits for individuals in restricted areas: **1¼ rem per calendar quarter** to the whole body, **18¾ rem per quarter** to the hands, forearms, feet, and ankles, and **7½ rem per quarter** to the skin of the whole body. An employer may permit up to **3 rem to the whole body in a quarter** only if the worker's accumulated occupational dose stays within **5(N−18) rem**, where N is age in years — and records prove it. There is no TEDE and no ALARA mandate; those concepts arrived with the modern 10 CFR Part 20 that OSHA never adopted.

So which rulebook binds a licensed gamma crew? **Section 4(b)(1) of the OSH Act** answers: OSHA stands down where another federal agency exercises its own statutory authority over working conditions — and a memorandum of understanding between the NRC and OSHA, first signed in 1988, draws the line in detail. For **licensed material, the NRC or Agreement State owns the radiological hazard**, and 1910.1096 itself closes the loop in paragraph (p): an employer that possesses and uses AEA material under an NRC or Agreement State license, in accordance with that regulator's standards, is **deemed in compliance** with OSHA's radiation rule for that possession and use.

The deemed-compliance bridge never covers what the license does not: **machine-produced radiation**. An X-ray crawler holds no licensed material, so worker protection there rests on the state's machine rules and on 1910.1096. On construction sites, **29 CFR 1926.53** points radiation work to 10 CFR Part 20-style standards and requires trained, competent persons to operate the equipment.

sections.caption: Two agencies, one job site: the materials license owns the radiological hazard of licensed sources, OSHA owns conventional hazards and machine radiation — and 29 CFR 1910.1096(p) deems a compliant licensee in compliance for the licensed material.

figures.title: From application to license — and staying licensed
NRC and OSHA: who protects the radiographer from what

interactive.title: Whose Rule Is It?
interactive.intro: Eight requirements land on one pipeline radiography job. Sort each one to the framework that imposes it: the radioactive materials license (NRC or Agreement State) or OSHA.

items.label: Maximum activity of Ir-192 the company may possess at any one time
items.why: Possession ceilings are conditions printed on the license itself — exceed them and the company holds unlicensed material.

items.label: Fall protection for radiographers shooting welds from a scaffold
items.why: Falls are a conventional workplace hazard — OSHA's general-industry and construction standards govern, and the materials license is silent.

items.label: Written operating and emergency procedures for source operations
items.why: 10 CFR 34.45 procedures are part of the 34.13 application package, and the tie-down condition makes them binding license requirements.

items.label: Permit-required confined-space entry before radiographing inside a vessel
items.why: Confined-space entry is 29 CFR 1910.146 — an OSHA program of permits, atmosphere testing, and attendants that no radiation regulator administers.

items.label: Worker dose limits on an X-ray crawler job with no licensed material in use
items.why: A machine holds no AEA material, so the NRC has no authority; OSHA's 29 CFR 1910.1096 quarterly limits (with state machine rules) protect the operator.

items.label: Naming and qualifying the Radiation Safety Officer
items.why: The RSO is identified on the license with qualifications per 10 CFR 34.42 — changing the RSO requires a license amendment.

items.label: Hazard communication and safety data sheets for film-processing chemicals
items.why: Darkroom chemistry is a chemical hazard under OSHA's HazCom standard, 29 CFR 1910.1200 — labels, SDS access, and training.

items.label: Observing each radiographer's job performance at least every 6 months
items.why: The semiannual job-performance inspection program of 10 CFR 34.43 is committed in the license application and enforced as a license condition.

checkQuestions.question: A company wants to perform industrial radiography with Ir-192. Under what authorization may that work be done?
options: A general license acquired automatically when the exposure devices are purchased
A state X-ray machine registration covering all of the company's radiation work
Only a specific license issued by the NRC or an Agreement State after review of a complete application
An OSHA permit issued under 29 CFR 1910.1096
checkQuestions.explanation: Industrial radiography never qualifies for a general license — 10 CFR 34.13 requires a specific license, granted only after the regulator reviews the full application package against 10 CFR 30.33 and Part 34. Machine registration covers X-ray equipment only, and OSHA does not license radioactive material at all.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of items must an applicant submit under 10 CFR 34.13 for a radiography license?
options: A radiographer training program per §34.43, written operating and emergency procedures per §34.45, and the RSO's qualifications per §34.42
Proof of liability insurance, a customer list, and medical examinations for each radiographer
Only the radionuclides and maximum activities requested, since procedures are reviewed during inspections
ASNT NDT Level III certificates in radiographic testing for every radiographer
checkQuestions.explanation: Section 34.13 requires the whole safety program up front: training and certification-verification procedures, operating and emergency procedures, the semiannual job-performance inspection program, organizational structure, RSO qualifications, in-house leak-test and calibration methods, and locations of use and records. Insurance, customer lists, and NDT Level III certificates are not Part 34 licensing requirements.

checkQuestions.question: A licensee's application stated that crews would document surveys on a specific company form. An inspector finds crews using a different, undocumented format. Why is this citable even though no dose limit was exceeded?
options: It is not citable — internal procedures are voluntary guidance
Because OSHA's General Duty Clause requires standardized forms
Because survey documentation formats are prescribed by ASNT
Because the statements and procedures in the application are incorporated into the license as binding conditions, so deviating from them violates the license
checkQuestions.explanation: A standard tie-down condition makes the application — including its procedures — part of the license. Drifting from what was submitted is license noncompliance regardless of dose consequences; the fix is to amend the license before changing the practice.

checkQuestions.question: A gamma crew works under an Agreement State license and complies fully with the state's 10 CFR Part 20-equivalent radiation rules. What is the crew's status under OSHA's ionizing radiation standard, 29 CFR 1910.1096, for that source?
options: The employer must independently meet OSHA's quarterly limits and keep a separate set of OSHA dose records
The employer is deemed in compliance with 1910.1096 with respect to the possession and use of the licensed material
The crew is exempt from every OSHA requirement on the job site
The crew is in violation, because 1910.1096 is more restrictive than modern Part 20 rules
checkQuestions.explanation: Paragraph (p) of 1910.1096 deems NRC and Agreement State licensees who comply with their regulator's standards to be in compliance with OSHA's radiation rule for that licensed material. The exemption covers only the radiological hazard of the licensed source — falls, confined spaces, and other conventional hazards remain fully OSHA's.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.42, the Radiation Safety Officer for an industrial radiography licensee must have, among other qualifications:
options: An ASNT NDT Level III certificate in radiographic testing
A bachelor's degree in health physics

Completion of radiographer training and testing requirements, 2,000 hours of hands-on experience as a qualified radiographer, and formal training in establishing and maintaining a radiation safety program
Ten years of service as a radiographer's assistant
checkQuestions.explanation: Part 34 builds the RSO from the radiographer up: the same training and examinations required of radiographers, 2,000 hours of hands-on experience as a qualified radiographer, and formal training in running a radiation safety program. No degree or NDT Level III certificate is required by the rule.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for industrial radiography and radiation safety requirements for industrial radiographic operations (esp. §§34.11–34.13, 34.25, 34.27, 34.42–34.45)
10 CFR Part 30, §30.33 — general requirements for issuance of specific licenses; NUREG-1556 Vol. 2 (industrial radiography license application guidance)
Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970; 29 CFR 1910.1096 (ionizing radiation) and 29 CFR 1926.53 (construction)
CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §12.3–12.4 (radioactive materials license requirements; OSHA)
